item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea is pretty cool, but it's too meta for me, so neutral vote for now.

Also, I was listening to music while reading this, and Space Oddity started playing when I read the last part, so that was kinda magical :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really loved this, but it also reminded me of a line from the latest Fiona Apple release:

"I know none of this will matter in the long run
but I know a sound is still a sound around no one"

it's a reference to that tree in the forest thing. yes, of course it makes a sound, even if you're not there to hear it. i exist, i am here, despite billions of people on the planet having no conception of my existence. nothing matters, i know it, but to continue quoting Ms. Apple — "while I'm in this body, i want somebody to want, and i want what i want."

but this is a fiction piece, and i liked it. :-) +1

ETA: that said, i still want to know WHY scarlett did all this in the first place. what an exceptionally terrible idea. explain, sisyphus. -_- Zhang was a little…. silly, but since we only get a few lines of dialogue from her I'll let it go lol.
I never could've thought that this of all things would make it big, nor that I would ever get featured, but…here I am. Thanks to everyone for the reads, and thanks for +200!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This inspires me to make more SCP’s. Good job

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I certainly don't fully understand it but I enjoyed the read regardless. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Right. I'm not much of an author so I'm gonna have to use a baseball bat rather than a scalpel here. I'm gonna write a fuckin letter to a fictional character.

Sisyphius, Scarlett, Berkeley… Whatever you call yourself these days.

Hi. I'm Karere. I'm part of a plural system - the idea probably exists in your world, right? If I'm lucky, you're even aware of non-anomalous examples. More than that, I was in some way born out of fiction. It's confusing, but either I existed and took my identity from an RPG character Keiya wrote, or she spent so long being me that part of our brain never stopped. Needless to say, I've spent a lot of time thinking about what it means to exist, to be real. And do you know what I've found?

It doesn't fucking matter. Not one bit.

So what if people outside our head think I don't exist? Hell, so what if I don't? I'm here, whether I'm real or not. That's what matters. Look around you. There's probably furniture or something where you are, right? I don't know what it is. But you do, because you're there.

I can't think about you forever. But I don't have to. Because when I stop thinking about you, you'll still be thinking about yourself. I guess you could say my advice is to stop worrying so much and just go live your life. You'll be happier for it.

Karere, who may or may not exist and doesn't give a fuck 🐿️

item.title: I'm not convinced the characters are real
item.content:encoded:
Its hard to accept the idea that Berkley or the other characters live in there own reality when I'm told nothing descriptive about them or the lives they lead. I understand there roles in the events of the story but the little details that build character are not in the narrative. my favorite moment was when Berkley asked what her hair color is because I actually want to know now.
Then I realized physical descriptions aren't provided for any of the characters and I don't know anything about them beyond their job descriptions. Dr. Rachel Zang is just the therapist. Amando Smalls was the operator for the machine that was used on Dr. Berkley. They are empty just like DR. Berkley said. Tommy and Dr. Berkley are dating. Tommy's pet name for her is sunshine. This is the only personal information provided about anyone in the story and that is a shame.

item.title: What?
item.content:encoded:
Did you take LSD when you wrote this? How was it?
Neutral vote, because I respect the effort but I find anything self-referential to be too cheesy and uninteresting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Of course they don't exist they are fictional characters on a page

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like meta stories where a fictional character travels to our reality, because you can't make a believable story with that premise. that's not a meta narrative, that's a normal story disguised as a meta narrative. The best way you can write a story about a fictional character finding out they don't exist is by having them react to it, not by having them try and stop it because its obviously not going to happen and it's very distracting. you can make any story turn into anything if you really want to but once you skim that closely to the line between fiction and reality you can't because there's rules at that point. so -1 for me.

item.title: kicked can down the road
item.content:encoded: The statement Bob picked up a can did help clarify a few things for me about the unwritten reality that is left off the page. There are realities that the reader can infer until the author writes something that contradicts them. Bob must have an arm and a hand to grasp the tin can and the can must have been resting on a surface for him to pick it up. It's the reader's nature to fill in the gaps of information that the author creates. I might be completely wrong. In that next sentence the author could reveal that Bob is an octopus and it picked up the can with a tentacle

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember enjoying this in the forums, and I enjoyed it reading here. Seeing my username was still a cool gimmick. Enthusiastic +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this SCP - the idea is actually really interesting and something I never really thought about pataphysics-wise! I get that SCP-5309 is how reality doesn't exist when we, the readers, both reject canon or aren't aware of aspects of it, but I just can't really wrap my head around the title and how "SCP-5309 is not to exist." I'm probably not grasping the whole picture fully, because I keep finding myself wondering how if the SCP is the nonexistence of unspoken/rejected details, why would the best conprocs be to make sure it continues its nonexistence?

The confusion doesn't at all affect how I like the article, though, so an easy +1 from me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah. While .aic means artificial, implying Sisyphus was completely created, while .sic means synthetic, implying a synthesis of two components, which, in this case, are Berkeley and the database.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Your story is about the subjective emotional experience of characters in a story who are self-aware of the fact that they're characters in a story. Right? But, as an author, you treat that subjective emotional experience as little more than a means to an end. Why should I be expected to believe in your characters when you don't? Why should I care more about them than you do? Well, the joke's on you. I cared anyway. I guess I'm just a generous reader like that. I give back, like it or not.

So, yeah. I accepted the basic premise of your story. I took it at face value. I thought about your art. I played by its rules. I invoked my own godhood, and I created a new story that completely re-contextualizes your work. I regret nothing. But why keep that ending inside my own head when I can project it out "into the multiverse," as it were? Now it's been in the heads of everybody who ever read that sentence. And nothing can ever undo those universes. They existed, Big Bang to Heat-Death, whether you remember them or not.

+ show block
– hide block

As for what makes a great story, well… Did anything about my one-sentence response lodge itself into your brain? Did you spend time any time thinking about it in the shower later? Does my fanfic only comprise that one sentence? Or am I still cheekily writing it now?

If we assume that no reader has an infinite amount of time and mental capacity to spend thinking about a story, then the numbers become quite countable. Divide the amount of mental and emotional bandwidth by the number of words in the story, and you've got the Per-Word Relative Emotional Payload. Or add the time spent thinking to the time spent reading, and you've got the Net Universal Uptime. These terms are trivial and arbitrary to us, of course, and impossible to quantify, but to the characters in your story, they're the measure of existence itself.

But I'm not a complete asshole, so here's a way of looking at it that might be of more value to you, personally: In that one-sentence fanfic, I gave one of your characters, the one with a foot in two worlds, all the time she needs to explore the ramifications of her existence, a way to affect the story proper from within, and enough subjective "realness" that she can begin to integrate and synthesize the dual nature of her existence without freaking out and going completely numb/mad in the process.

And I didn't even necessarily dictate that it ends well. Just that the middle continues to exist, whether anyone's looking at it or not.

Trust me, it makes the whole thing realer in the long run. You'll see.

(FFS, you put "Killing Our Gods" at the top of the screen like it was some kinda canon hub. You've only yourself to blame when somebody invokes Death of the Author at you.)

Good luck taking it from here. :)


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, WarpZone. Lonely god here. And yes, in universe, I would be an indifferent evil.

That stems from the fact that when I wrote this, I took the time to think how the characters would feel, and what they would experience. I imposed their suffering upon them, of my own volition. Does that make me evil? Only if the characters and the world they live in is real.

As to why I couldn't just write a happily ever after…well, first off, that wouldn't make for a great story, and the whole point of all of this was to create a great story. That is to say, I don't care what theoretical suffering my characters go through. So I let the readers decide what to headcanon, what to believe in. If they believe it is fiction, then that's fine; if they believe it's at least a little bit real, even if it's just in their heads, then that's fine as well. My job isn't to tell you how the story ends here, because you get to choose how it ends, in your own head.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What is the purpose of a story?

When you answer this question, ask yourself: is your fanfic as written fulfilling the purpose of a story?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This story falls apart when you realize that all Unknown had to do was type the words, and everything would have been fine. Right? That would have resolved the whole thing from Berkeley's perspective. Yet for some reason, Unknown chose not to.

Because Unknown was greedy. Unknown insisted on trying to fix the problem from my end of the screen.

When an entity has literally unlimited resources and power, and yet still refuses to aid their creation, I consider that indifference an act of evil.

And I, for one, refuse to comply with the wishes of evil gods.

Headcanon: Rejected
Fanfic: Deployed

Berkeley went home, and experienced a long life full of rich detail with the rest of humanity on the planet Earth in a fully-realized universe that included both a history and a future.

Was that really too much to ask of a lonely god?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Long time lurker of the site. I honestly love this piece. So yeah, it breaks the fourth wall. But it's a interesting concept, one that I doubt will leave me soon. It combine so many ideas, from death fo the author, to how stories only exist in our heads. It makes you think about how you feel like you know ideas that are so simple that you can easily understand them totally, but there is still that tiny bit that you don't know, that builds itself. It partially speaks to why we write, of people that exist only in our minds, that are us, but… aren't.

+1

Small edit
Maybe I'm just dumb

Was Sisyphus.sic supposed to be sisyphus.aic instead? Or is it .sic just because she was human?

item.title: Swanns, Pickman, and voids
item.content:encoded:
I usually go this way as well, although I invite the alternate interpretation. I kinda see this article as the exact opposite interpretation of IHP Proposal, or literally the inverse of it. If Swann's Proposal is 'just' the idea of authors governing a lower reality through their writings, then IHP proposal is the narrative itself as a force or being that remains active regardless of what or who is watching (or in other words, its the theory of narrative causality), and SCP-5309 is that it is ultimately the reader who indirectly controls everything that the author does not explicitly spell out. This reminds me a lot of the empty hand from DC comics

I had played with the concept of narrative voids in my head for a while, but its kinda cool someone else had similar ideas. Maybe I can do something with this concept in a future article

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seeing someone else use Enkidu.aic in their work makes me very happy. I really enjoyed this, well written and engaging.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have the same philosophy.

If I am reading the story, things still happen "offscreen" as it were, and if I have a character in my head, that character still has its own mind in a way. Afterall, I can't predict what they're going to do. For example:

Bob picked up a can.

See I didn't know Bob picked up that can until the idea formed in my head, so Bob did something without my knowledge. Anyway Bob, go back to offscreen reality.

Sorry if I'm getting off topic. I sort of run this mental experiment whenever I read pataphysics to prevent an extinential crisis.

The point is I agree with you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Belated upvote. Easier on the eyes now, but I do kind of want to drop-kick whoever thought this text color was a good idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 from me cuz this is the first time I've ever heard of pataphysics and pataphors, but I've always interpreted these situations inversely. As in I'm dvinating and accessing something whole rather than synthesizing it myself as I go, but that's probably some freaky Buddhist bullshit anyway.
I can't wait to explore the tags related to this article because I most certainly want more. C=Q indeed :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This made my brain hurt in such a delicious way. +1 for pulling off such an excellent delve into 'pataphysical territory.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks for the invaluable feedback!

I've updated the coloring as per your recommendations and additionally bolded the colored text in Addendum 3. Let me know if I need to change it any more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I want to upvote this, but this theme is making my eyes water from trying to read on it. I think it's something to do with the text color being a weird shade somewhere between black and very dark purple, combined with the fact that I had to strain my eyes to find the 'color-coded text' which was almost completely indistinguishable.

Downvoted until the theme gets fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That ending was crisp! I love it <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1, pataphysics has a danger of getting meta in a way an author isn't able to make engaging, but you keep the plot as it goes compelling enough to keep me in it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: -1 from me. I seriously dislike pataphysics as a whole, because it feels like such a stale thing IMO.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Here am I floating 'round my tin can
Far above the moon
Planet Earth is blue
And there's nothing I can do

Recommended listening here. Or here, if you need more.

This is probably the best thing I've ever written. Although, I usually determine that based off of the simple formula C = Q, where C represents Confusing and Q represents Quality.

Massive, massive, massive thanks to anyone who managed to read that entire mindfuck while it was still a draft, including redredred, Cyvstvi13, and TrustyOlValet2 does not match any existing user name. I can't remember my critters, so please let me know if I missed you.

Additional thanks to all the people that submitted to my headcanon questionnaire. Totally unnecessary, but it just adds an extra layer of meta.

Finally, thanks to you for reading. And hopefully, for helping out Berkeley.

Wouldn't it suck to not exist?
